sanskrit_devanagari:
भोगैश्वर्यप्रसक्तानां तयापहृतचेतसाम् ।
व्यवसायात्मिका बुद्धिः समाधौ न विधीयते ॥ ४४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
bhogaiśvarya-prasaktānāṁ tayāpahṛta-cetasām |
vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ samādhau na vidhīyate || 44 ||

exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.44।।)
IAST exact (bhogaiśvarya-prasaktānāṁ tayāpahṛta-cetasām vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ samādhau na vidhīyate)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.044). IAST body identical: 'bhogaiśvaryaprasaktānāṃ tayāpahṛtacetasām / vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ samādhau na vidhīyate'.

anvaya.meaning: of those (who are) deeply attached / clinging
by that (refers back to puṣpitā vāc of 2.42)
of those whose discriminating mind is stolen / carried away
of the nature of resolution / one-pointed in determination

translations.verbatim_quote: In the minds of those who are too attached to sense enjoyment and material opulence, and who are bewildered by such things, the resolute determination for devotional service to the Supreme Lord does not take place.
With their minds deeply attached to worldly pleasures and their intellects bewildered by such things, they are unable to possess the resolute determination for success on the path to God.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Samādhi means "fixed mind." The Vedic dictionary, the Nirukti, says, samyag ādhīyate 'sminn ātma-tattva-yāthātmyam: "When the mind is fixed for understanding the self, it is said to be in samādhi." Samādhi is never possible for persons interested in material sense enjoyment and bewildered by such temporary things.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.44 And vyavasayatmika, one-pointed; buddhih, conviction, with regard to Knowledge or Yoga; na vidhiyate, does not become established, i.e. does not arise; samadhau, in the minds — bhoga-aisvarya-prasaktanam, of those who delight in enjoyment and wealth, of those who have the hankering that only enjoyment as also wealth is to be sought for; and apahrta-cetasam, of those whose intellects are carried away, whose discriminating judgement becomes covered; taya, by that speech which is full of various special rites.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 2.44 as the consequence-clause of 2.42-43: those whose attention has been captured (apahṛta-cetas — discriminating judgement covered over) by the flowery kāmya-vāda — and who as a result are prasakta (clinging) to bhoga and aiśvarya — cannot generate vyavasāyātmikā buddhi in samādhi. The point is causal: the attachment captures the cetas (discriminating mind), which prevents the samādhi-collected state, which is the condition for the resolute one-pointed buddhi to arise. Crucially, samādhi here is read by Shankara as the collected mental state in which Knowledge can be received — it is not yet the eighth-limb samādhi of Patañjali, but the prerequisite gathered attention. The blocker is named precisely: status-attainment-attachment (bhoga-aiśvarya-prasakti) is what eliminates the conditions for resolute insight.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use; closing segment of joint 2.42-44 bhāṣya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: (continued from 2.43) ... Their minds being thus carried away by the speech which describes the rituals leading to enjoyment and power, the Buddhi of resolution concerning the realisation of the self does not get established in their minds (samadhi means here a calmed mind). The Buddhi which arises out of right knowledge of the self, mentioned earlier in verse 41, does not arise in them, because their minds are agitated by attachment to fleeting objects.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads samādhi explicitly as 'a calmed mind' — the necessary condition for vyavasāyātmikā buddhi to take root. He explicitly back-references 2.41: the resolute buddhi of 2.41 does not arise in those of 2.42-43 because their minds are agitated by attachment to fleeting (anitya) objects. The chain is: speech captures cetas → cetas clings to bhoga / aiśvarya → calmed-mind state cannot form → resolute buddhi cannot establish. The Vishishtadvaita reading specifically identifies the blocker as attachment to fleeting objects (not the rituals themselves), preserving the kāmya-karma's validity when performed without that attachment.

positions.rendering: the resolute determination for devotional service to the Supreme Lord does not take place
they are unable to possess the resolute determination for success on the path to God
vyavasāyātmikā buddhi (with regard to Knowledge or Yoga) does not become established in samādhi (the collected mental state)
the Buddhi of resolution does not get established; samādhi here means a calmed mind

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The phrase 'samādhau na vidhīyate' is grammatically about state-establishment: the resolute buddhi does not get located/established in samādhi. The disagreement is what samādhi means here. Prabhupada localises the resolute buddhi to 'devotional service to the Supreme Lord' specifically; Mukundananda generalises to 'success on the path to God.' Shankara reads samādhi as the collected mental state in which Knowledge (jñāna) or yoga can take root — Advaita-flavoured but not the technical eighth-limb samādhi yet. Ramanuja explicitly glosses samādhi as 'calmed mind' — i.e., the condition where the buddhi can be established. All four read the verse causally: attachment to bhoga/aiśvarya prevents the samādhi-state, which prevents the buddhi from establishing. The doctrinal point is not 'devotion is hard' but 'attention-capture by status-attainment-attachment is the specific blocker'.

literal_meaning: Of those who are deeply attached to enjoyment and lordship, whose discriminating mind has been carried away by that (flowery speech), the resolute intelligence is not established in samādhi.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 2.44 names the consequence of the 2.42-43 critique: the avipaścitaḥ described there have a specific cognitive failure mode — their cetas (discriminating mind) is captured (apahṛta) by the flowery kāmya-vāda, they cling (prasakta) to bhoga (enjoyment) and aiśvarya (lordship), and as a result vyavasāyātmikā buddhi (the resolute one-pointed intelligence introduced in 2.41) cannot establish itself in samādhi (the collected/calmed mental state). The verse is a causal chain: attachment captures attention → attention-capture blocks the samādhi precondition → without samādhi, the resolute buddhi cannot arise. Shankara and Ramanuja agree on the chain; they differ slightly on what samādhi means here (Shankara: collected mind ready for Knowledge; Ramanuja: calmed mind). The verse is doctrinally important because it names attachment-to-status-fruits as a focus-blocker — not as a moral failure but as a structural impossibility for the cognitive state required.

cross_references.explanation: vyavasāyātmikā buddhi was introduced at 2.41 as the one-pointed intelligence of those on the path; 2.44 names what prevents it from arising. The two verses bookend the buddhi-yoga gateway.
The 'tayā' (by that) at 2.44 refers back to the puṣpitā vāc (flowery speech) introduced at 2.42. 2.44 cannot be read without 2.42's antecedent.
The 'bhoga-aiśvarya' compound at 2.44 ('bhogaiśvarya-prasaktānām') directly continues 2.43's 'bhogaiśvarya-gatim prati'. Ramanuja in particular treats 2.42-44 as one bhāṣya unit.

verbatim_quote_sources: bhagavad-gita.us (Ramanuja Gita-bhasya, Adidevananda tr., closing segment of joint 2.42-44 bhāṣya)
